name: Sheridan, Thomas B.
recent_1: Sheridan, T. B. (2020). A Distracted Scientist: The Life and Contributions of John Senders. Human Factors, 62(8), 1219-1228. DOI: 10.1177/0018720820941970
recent_2: Sheridan, T. B. (2019). Extending Three Existing Models to Analysis of Trust in Automation: Signal Detection, Statistical Parameter Estimation, and Model-Based Control. Human Factors, 61(7), 1017-1023. DOI: 10.1177/0018720819829951
recent_3: Sheridan, T. B. (2017). The Intricacies of User Adjustments of Alerting Thresholds. Human Factors, 59(6), 901-910. DOI: 10.1177/0018720817698616
recent_1_synopsis: A tribute and intellectual biography of John Senders, recounting his life and his foundational contributions to attention sampling, visual workload, and driver distraction research. Beyond memorializing a colleague, it traces the lineage of key human factors ideas (e.g., the queueing/sampling models of attention) that remain central to studies of distraction and monitoring. It situates Senders's work within the broader development of the field.
recent_2_synopsis: This paper recasts trust in automation through three established quantitative frameworks—signal detection theory, statistical parameter estimation, and model-based control—to give trust a more formal, analyzable structure. It argues that treating trust as a measurable decision variable allows designers to predict reliance and calibrate automation transparency. The work matters because it pushes the largely descriptive trust literature toward testable, model-driven analysis.
recent_3_synopsis: Sheridan analyzes how operators set and adjust alerting/alarm thresholds, exposing the trade-offs between false alarms and missed detections that govern user trust and response. Using a signal-detection lens, he shows why naive threshold choices can degrade joint human-automation performance. The paper provides design guidance for adaptive alerting systems where the operator, not just the engineer, tunes sensitivity.
seminal_1: Parasuraman, R., Sheridan, T. B., & Wickens, C. D. (2000). A model for types and levels of human interaction with automation. IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics - Part A: Systems and Humans, 30(3), 286-297. DOI: 10.1109/3468.844354
seminal_2: Sheridan, T. B. (1992). Telerobotics, Automation, and Human Supervisory Control. Cambridge, MA: MIT Press. ISBN: 978-0-262-19316-7 (no Crossref DOI)
seminal_1_synopsis: This landmark paper proposes a framework distinguishing four broad functions that can be automated (information acquisition, information analysis, decision selection, and action implementation) and a continuum of levels of automation within each. Approximately 6,300 citations (Google Scholar, June 2026), it is one of the most cited works in human factors and the standard vocabulary for describing where and how much to automate. It is foundational because it gave designers and researchers a shared, analyzable model for allocating functions between humans and machines.
seminal_2_synopsis: This MIT Press monograph is the definitive synthesis of Sheridan's pioneering work on supervisory control and telerobotics, formalizing the idea that humans increasingly supervise rather than directly operate complex automated systems. Approximately 3,700 citations (Google Scholar, June 2026), it defined the conceptual foundations—supervisory control loops, telepresence, and human-automation function allocation—that underpin modern research on remote operation, robotics, and automated vehicles. It is foundational as the canonical reference establishing supervisory control as a distinct field.
expertise_overview: Sheridan's research centers on human supervisory control: how human operators plan, monitor, intervene in, and trust automated and semi-automated systems rather than controlling them continuously. Core topics include levels and types of automation, function allocation between humans and machines, telerobotics and teleoperation, telepresence and virtual presence, and trust and reliance in automation. Methodologically he favors analytical and mathematical modeling—control theory, signal detection theory, estimation theory, queueing/attention-sampling models—to formalize otherwise descriptive human-machine concepts. Application domains span aviation and air traffic, nuclear and process control, undersea and space telerobotics, surface transportation and automated driving, and alerting/alarm system design. His work also engages alerting thresholds, operator situation awareness, and mode transitions in automation. Across these threads he emphasizes quantitative, testable models of joint human-automation performance.
notes: Identification confidence: HIGH. This is Thomas B. Sheridan, Professor Emeritus of Mechanical Engineering and Aeronautics/Astronautics at MIT, founding figure in supervisory control and telerobotics. Identity confirmed via Wikipedia, MIT/ILP faculty listing, ResearchGate (Thomas-Sheridan-5), Research.com profile, and Google Scholar (user skzsFMUAAAAJ), cross-checked against the four pre-verified Human Factors anchor papers (2016-2020), all of which list T. B. Sheridan of MIT. DOI verification (all via Crossref api.crossref.org, June 17 2026, HTTP 200 with matching titles): recent_1 10.1177/0018720820941970 (verified), recent_2 10.1177/0018720819829951 (verified), recent_3 10.1177/0018720817698616 (verified), seminal_1 10.1109/3468.844354 (verified; title and authors Parasuraman/Sheridan/Wickens confirmed). The three 'recent' papers are his most recent verifiable peer-reviewed journal articles; as an emeritus author his latest journal work is from 2020 (the John Senders tribute). Post-2020 output is chiefly book chapters (e.g., 'Human Supervisory Control of Automation,' Handbook of Human Factors and Ergonomics, 2021) and books ('Modeling Human-System Interaction,' Wiley, 2017). UNVERIFIED DOI: seminal_2, the 1992 MIT Press book 'Telerobotics, Automation, and Human Supervisory Control,' has NO Crossref DOI of its own (Crossref returns only book-review records, e.g., 10.1017/s0263574700016209 and 10.1016/0005-1098(94)90205-4, which are reviews, not the book); cited with ISBN 978-0-262-19316-7 instead. Citation counts (~6,300 for seminal_1; ~3,700 for the 1992 book) are from Google Scholar, accessed June 17 2026, and are approximate. An alternative high-impact seminal paper with a verified DOI is Sheridan's 'Musings on Telepresence and Virtual Presence,' Presence 1(1), 1992, DOI 10.1162/pres.1992.1.1.120 (verified, ~2,400 cites), should a fully DOI-backed second seminal be preferred over the book.
